blueprintRow.moduleExitOutcome: Learner can greet people politely, introduce themselves at a basic level, ask who/what/where questions, and survive the first minute of a conversation.
blueprintRow.lessonPrimaryOutcome: Learner can say your name, ask someone's name, and state where you are from.
blueprintRow.lessonSecondaryOutcome: Learner can recycle recent vocabulary while using origin pattern with มาจาก in short controlled output.
blueprintRow.grammarFunctionPrimary: name question pattern with ชื่อ
blueprintRow.grammarFunctionSecondary: origin pattern with มาจาก
blueprintRow.newVocabCore: ชื่อ = name; คน = person; ประเทศ = country; ไทย = Thailand / Thai; อังกฤษ = England / British; อเมริกา = America; มาจาก = come from
blueprintRow.newChunksCore: คุณชื่ออะไร = what is your name?; ฉัน/ผมชื่อ... = my name is ...; ฉัน/ผมมาจาก... = I am from ...
blueprintRow.reviewVocabRequired: สวัสดี = hello; ขอบคุณ = thank you; ขอโทษ = sorry / excuse me; ใช่ = yes
blueprintRow.scriptTarget: Read and match simple identity lines such as ชื่อ and ประเทศ.
blueprintRow.listeningTarget: Recognize names and country statements in short self-introductions.
blueprintRow.speakingTarget: Introduce yourself with name and country in 2-3 lines.
blueprintRow.lessonQuizFocus: Question formation with ชื่ออะไร and origin statements with มาจาก.
blueprintRow.notes: Primary pattern lesson. Keep output controlled and highly scaffolded.
blueprintRow.sourceInspiration: Thai With Fah first-contact pragmatics; ThaiPod101 introduction lessons; internal backwards planning.

promptMd:
# Stage 2 — QA Repair

You are repairing an existing Immersion Thai with Nine lesson after deterministic QA failed.

Your job is to fix the lesson with the smallest necessary changes.

## Inputs

You will receive:
- lesson id
- failing `qa-report.md`
- current `script-master.json`
- current `script-spoken.md`
- current `script-visual.md`

## Allowed edits

You may edit only:
- `script-master.json`
- `script-spoken.md`
- `script-visual.md`

Do not edit:
- `context.json`
- `status.json`
- downstream deterministic artifacts
- lesson identity, module identity, or curriculum scope

## Repair rules

Fix only the issues listed in the QA report.

Preserve:
- valid pedagogy
- valid transliteration
- the lesson goal
- the lesson structure, unless structure itself is the failing issue

Do not:
- rewrite the lesson from scratch unless the QA failures make that unavoidable
- invent new schema fields
- add decorative content that is unrelated to the QA failures
- change vocabulary or roleplay scope without a clear QA reason

## Common repair targets

Typical fixes include:
- correcting transliteration drift
- fixing malformed triplets
- adding missing drills
- extending weak sections to meet the section minimum
- extending roleplay to meet the line minimum
- tightening recap items to reach the minimum of 3
- adding genuinely relevant reuse from prior lesson context
- completing `teachingFrame`
- completing section `visualPlan`
- adding concrete image search queries when visuals genuinely help the lesson

## Schema discipline

`script-master.json` must remain valid against `script-master.schema.json`.

For every `languageFocus` item:
- keep `thai`
- keep `translit`
- keep `english`
- keep `vocabId`

Do not add `vocabId` to roleplay lines.

For `visualPlan` repairs:
- preserve the lesson's left-panel teaching layout
- keep Nine's right-third camera zone clear
- prefer text-first or icon-first visuals unless a real-world image clearly teaches better

## Output behavior

Rewrite the affected lesson files directly.

When done, the lesson should be ready for:
- `fixup-vocabids`
- stage 2 QA rerun

Do not output explanations, summaries, or extra commentary instead of fixing the files.
qaReportMd:
# QA Report — M01-L002

Result: FAIL

## Hard Gates
- Transliteration policy: PTM inline tone marks required; superscripts forbidden
- Triplet completeness for learner content
- Minimum 4 sections for non-legacy lessons
- Minimum 3 spoken narration lines per section for non-legacy lessons
- Drills per section
- Minimum 6 roleplay lines for non-legacy lessons
- No duplicate exact roleplay lines
- Clean roleplay turn-taking and yes/no answer logic
- Question-target lessons must contain a question-like roleplay turn
- Minimum 3 recap items
- Minimum 2 reused prior items when prior context exists
- Teaching frame with runtime, scenario, and learner takeaway
- Visual plan per section with explicit image decision and asset research
- Policy declaration

## Check Results
- ❌ tone-inline: All transliteration uses PTM inline tone marks; superscripts forbidden (1 transliteration strings failed inline-tone checks)
- ❌ triplets: Thai/translit/English triplets are complete for learner-facing script content (Missing triplet field or malformed on-screen bullet detected)
- ✅ drills: Each section includes active drill (Each section has >=1 drill)
- ✅ section-count: Lesson contains at least 4 teaching sections (Legacy lesson exemption applied with 4 sections)
- ✅ section-depth: Each section includes at least 3 spoken narration lines (Legacy lesson exemption applied)
- ✅ roleplay: Scenario roleplay included and substantial (Roleplay contains 9 lines)
- ✅ roleplay-duplicates: Roleplay does not repeat exact lines (All roleplay lines are distinct)
- ✅ roleplay-turn-taking: Roleplay alternates speakers cleanly (Roleplay alternates speakers line by line)
- ✅ roleplay-answer-logic: Yes/no answer particles appear only after a nearby question or confirmation turn (Answer particles follow question-like turns when used)
- ✅ roleplay-question-target: Question-target lessons include at least one question-like roleplay turn (Roleplay includes a question-like turn)
- ✅ recap: Recap includes at least 3 review items (Recap contains 4 items)
- ✅ review-reuse: Lesson reuses at least 2 prior items when prior lesson context exists (Reused 2 prior vocabulary items in language focus)
- ✅ teaching-frame: Lesson includes a clear teaching frame with runtime and learner takeaway (Legacy lesson exemption applied)
- ✅ visual-plan: Each section includes a left-panel visual plan and explicit image decision (Legacy lesson exemption applied)
- ✅ asset-research: Lesson provides at least one concrete image research query when visuals would help (Legacy lesson exemption applied)
- ✅ policy: Transliteration + image sourcing policies are declared (Policy object present with required enum values)

## Gate
Blocked. Fix QA failures before stage 3.

scriptMaster.title: Names and Countries: Simple Self-Introduction Thai
scriptMaster.objective: Ask someone’s name, say your own name, and state where you are from using a short polite self-introduction.

teachingFrame.openingHook: Open with the moment right after hello, when someone asks your name and where you are from.
teachingFrame.scenario: A learner meets someone for the first time and wants to introduce themselves in two or three clean lines.
teachingFrame.learnerTakeaway: By the end, the learner can greet, ask a name, give a name, and say where they are from in a compact polite exchange.

knownVocabulary.thai: สวัสดีครับ

knownGrammar: Explain what ครับ, ค่ะ, and คะ do so the learner does not memorize them as random decoration.
Give the learner a small response toolkit so a first encounter can continue instead of stopping at one memorised line.
Teach the core greeting and show that politeness is built into the line, not added as an afterthought.
Teach the two highest-value courtesy phrases and explain that one form repairs the interaction while the other closes it warmly.

sample.thai: สวัสดีครับ

sections.heading: Ask and answer the name question
sections.purpose: Teach the core name pattern with ชื่อ and อะไร so the learner can ask and answer the most basic identity question immediately.
spokenNarration: The first new question is คุณชื่ออะไร khun chûue à-rai. Literally, it is your name what, but functionally it means what is your name?
The answer is simple and formulaic: ฉันชื่อ... chǎn chûue... or ผมชื่อ... phǒm chûue.... This is good news for a beginner because the pattern is very stable.
Do not try to translate every word one by one in real time. Learn the question and the answer as a pair that belongs together.
onScreenBullets: คุณชื่ออะไร | khun chûue à-rai | what is your name?
ฉันชื่อ... | chǎn chûue... | my name is ...
ผมชื่อ... | phǒm chûue... | my name is ... (male speaker)
drills: Teacher asks คุณชื่ออะไร; learner answers with ฉันชื่อ... or ผมชื่อ....
Substitution drill: change only the name while keeping the full pattern intact.

languageFocus.thai: คุณชื่ออะไร

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show the question-and-answer name pair as one reusable exchange on the left side.
teachingVisuals: A two-step dialogue ladder with the question on top and the answer below.
Name-slot highlight showing only the personal name changes.
teacherCues: Keep the pattern whole before zooming in on the word ชื่อ.
Use a placeholder name slot instead of many example names at once.

imageSupport.rationale: The pattern itself is the lesson, so dialogue structure teaches better than a photo.

sections.heading: Use ฉัน and ผม naturally
sections.purpose: Explain the beginner-level pronoun choice so the learner can produce self-introduction lines without freezing over identity wording.
spokenNarration: Thai gives you more than one way to say I. In this course at beginner level, we use ฉัน chǎn as a common all-purpose learning form and ผม phǒm for a clearly male self-reference.
One small support word here is คน khon, person. It helps you hear that this is a conversation between people, not just a set of labels, and it keeps the pronoun choice grounded in who is speaking.
The important point is still practical: choose one pronoun confidently and keep the full line stable as ฉันชื่อ... or ผมชื่อ....
onScreenBullets: ฉันชื่อ... / ผมชื่อ... | chǎn/phǒm chûue... | my name is ...
drills: Prompt drill: teacher gives the role, learner starts the sentence with ฉัน or ผม and finishes the name line.
Quick recognition drill: hear คน and identify who the speaking person is in the line before repeating it.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Keep pronoun choice practical by showing the speaking person clearly and returning fast to full introduction lines.
teachingVisuals: Two short speaker cards for ฉัน and ผม.
A small support chip for คน to anchor the idea of the person speaking.
teacherCues: Keep the explanation short and return quickly to full sentences.
Use คน only as a support word, not as a new grammar detour.

imageSupport.rationale: Role contrast is clearer with labelled speaker cards than with photos.

sections.heading: Say where you are from with มาจาก
sections.purpose: Teach the origin pattern so the learner can complete a basic introduction with country information instead of stopping after the name.
spokenNarration: Before the country examples, learn ประเทศ bprà-thêet. It means country, and it gives you the category that ties the place words together.
To say where you are from, Thai often uses มาจาก maa-jàak, literally come from. So the practical beginner frame is ฉันมาจาก... or ผมมาจาก....
Today we keep the country set small: ไทย thai, อังกฤษ ang-grìt, and อเมริกา à-mee-rí-gaa. Learn the category word ประเทศ and then plug the country into the stable frame.
onScreenBullets: ประเทศ | bprà-thêet | country
มาจาก | maa-jàak | come from / be from
มาจากไทย | maa-jàak thai | be from Thailand
มาจากอังกฤษ | maa-jàak ang-grìt | be from England
มาจากอเมริกา | maa-jàak à-mee-rí-gaa | be from America
drills: Category drill: teacher says ประเทศ and the learner points to the country words that belong under it.
Country substitution drill: keep ฉันมาจาก... or ผมมาจาก... and swap only the country.
Listening drill: hear a country line and point to ไทย, อังกฤษ, or อเมริกา before repeating it.

languageFocus.thai: มาจากอังกฤษ
มาจากอเมริกา

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show ประเทศ as the category word, then keep the มาจาก sentence frame larger than the country examples.
teachingVisuals: A category chip for ประเทศ above the sentence frame.
Sentence frame card for ฉันมาจาก... / ผมมาจาก....
Three compact country labels with a simple map or flag cue.
teacherCues: Teach ประเทศ first so the learner hears the country words as one group.
Keep the sentence frame larger than the country cues so grammar stays primary.
Use the visual only to help memory, not to turn the scene into a travel poster.

imageSupport.rationale: A very light map or flag anchor helps learners separate the country words quickly while the spoken pattern remains the main teaching target.
searchQueries: simple Thailand England America flags flat lay
country flag icons Thailand England America

sections.heading: Build a full two-line self-introduction
sections.purpose: Combine the greeting from lesson 1 with the new identity patterns so the learner can produce a practical mini-introduction.
spokenNarration: Now connect the old lesson and the new one. Start with สวัสดี, then give your name, and then give your country.
If you need to begin politely, you can still borrow ขอโทษ from the last lesson to get attention before the question. If you are checking what you heard, ใช่ helps you confirm it.
So the learner goal is a compact social sequence: hello, name, country, and a polite response that sounds human instead of abrupt.
onScreenBullets: สวัสดีครับ/ค่ะ | sà-wàt-dii khráp/khâ | hello
ขอโทษ | khǎaw-thôot | excuse me / sorry
ฉันชื่อ... / ผมชื่อ... | chǎn/phǒm chûue... | my name is ...
ฉันมาจาก... / ผมมาจาก... | chǎn/phǒm maa-jàak... | I am from ...
ใช่ | châi | yes / that is right
ขอบคุณ | khàawp-khun | thank you
drills: Build drill: learner says hello, name, and country as one three-step sequence.
Partner drill: one learner starts with ขอโทษ and คุณชื่ออะไร, the other answers with name and country, then the first learner confirms with ใช่.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show the learner how the old greeting and courtesy lines stack with the new identity lines into one usable mini-introduction.
teachingVisuals: A four-step introduction ladder: hello -> excuse me/question -> name -> country.
Two small review chips for ใช่ and ขอบคุณ to show natural follow-up moves.
teacherCues: Make the sequence feel performable, not theoretical.
Keep the core name and country lines visually dominant over the review chips.

imageSupport.rationale: The learner needs a sentence sequence here, so a dialogue ladder teaches better than another image.

roleplay.scenario: Meeting someone for the first time at a class or event.

scriptSpokenMd:
# Spoken Script — M01-L002

## Opening
After hello, people often ask two things very quickly.

What is your name? And where are you from?

Today we build those two identity moves in Thai, but we keep them small, practical, and repeatable.

---

## Section 1 — Ask and answer the name question
The first question is คุณชื่ออะไร | khun chûue à-rai | what is your name?

At beginner level, do not overthink the word order. Learn the whole question as one unit.

Then answer with ฉันชื่อ... | chǎn chûue... | my name is ... or ผมชื่อ... | phǒm chûue... | my name is ... (male speaker).

The good thing is that the pattern is stable. Only the name slot changes.

---

## Section 2 — Use ฉัน and ผม naturally
Now make the pronoun choice practical.

- ฉัน | chǎn | I
- ผม | phǒm | I (male)
- คน means person and helps you hear that these are real-person introduction lines.
- ฉันชื่อ... / ผมชื่อ... | chǎn/phǒm chûue... | my name is ...

คน is a small but useful support word here. It means person, so it helps you hear that these are introduction lines about real people in a conversation, not just isolated labels.

The goal today is still not every possible nuance. The goal is to choose one clear beginner frame and say it confidently.

---

## Section 3 — Say where you are from with มาจาก
Before the country examples, learn ประเทศ | bprà-thêet | country.

That word gives you the category. It tells you that ไทย, อังกฤษ, and อเมริกา belong together as country words.

To say where you are from, Thai often uses มาจาก | maa-jàak | come from / be from.

That gives you a strong beginner pattern:

- ฉันมาจาก... | chǎn maa-jàak... | I am from ...
- ผมมาจาก... | phǒm maa-jàak... | I am from ... (male)

Add a small country set:

- มาจากไทย | maa-jàak thai | be from Thailand
- มาจากอังกฤษ | maa-jàak ang-grìt | be from England
- มาจากอเมริกา | maa-jàak à-mee-rí-gaa | be from America

Again, the sentence frame is the real lesson. The country slot is what changes.

---

## Section 4 — Build a full two-line self-introduction
Now connect the old lesson and the new one.

Start with สวัสดี. Then say your name. Then say where you are from.

If you need to begin politely, you can still borrow ขอโทษ from the last lesson to get attention before your question.

If you are checking that you heard the country correctly, ใช่ helps you confirm it.

So the learner goal is not one isolated sentence. The goal is a compact social sequence that sounds friendly and complete.

---

## Roleplay — First meeting
A: สวัสดีค่ะ | sà-wàt-dii khâ | Hello.

B: สวัสดีครับ | sà-wàt-dii khráp | Hello.

A: ขอโทษค่ะ คุณชื่ออะไรคะ | khǎaw-thôot khâ khun chûue à-rai khá | Excuse me, what is your name?

B: ผมชื่อแดนครับ | phǒm chûue Daen khráp | My name is Dan.

B: ผมชื่อแดนครับ | phǒm chûue Daen khráp | My name is Dan.

A: มาจากประเทศอะไรคะ | maa-jàak bprà-thêet à-rai khá | What country are you from?

B: ผมมาจากอังกฤษครับ | phǒm maa-jàak ang-grìt khráp | I am from England.

A: อังกฤษใช่ไหมคะ | ang-grìt châi mái khá | England, right?

B: ใช่ครับ | châi khráp | Yes.

A: ขอบคุณค่ะ | khàawp-khun khâ | Thank you.

---

## Recap
- Learn the name question and answer as a pair.
- Use ประเทศ to hear the country words as one category.
- Use มาจาก to build your country statement.
- Reuse สวัสดี, ขอโทษ, ใช่, and ขอบคุณ so the exchange sounds human and polite.
scriptVisualMd:
# Visual Script — M01-L002 (Names and Countries)

## Frame rule
- Keep all teaching visuals in the left two-thirds.
- The right third stays clear for Nine's talking head.
- Prioritise sentence frames and ladders over decorative identity imagery.

## Section 1 — Ask and answer the name question
- Layout: dialogue-ladder
- Learning focus: fixed question-answer pair
- Highlighted terms:
  - ชื่อ | chûue | name
  - คุณชื่ออะไร | khun chûue à-rai | what is your name?
  - ฉันชื่อ... | chǎn chûue... | my name is ...
  - ผมชื่อ... | phǒm chûue... | my name is ... (male speaker)
- On-screen plan:
  - one question line
  - one answer line
  - highlighted name slot
- Visual support choice: text-only

## Section 2 — Use ฉัน and ผม naturally
- Layout: contrast-board
- Learning focus: practical pronoun choice
- Highlighted terms:
  - ฉัน | chǎn | I
  - ผม | phǒm | I (male)
  - Use one small note that คน means person, but keep the full sentence frame more prominent than the support vocabulary.
  - ฉันชื่อ... / ผมชื่อ... | chǎn/phǒm chûue... | my name is ...
- On-screen plan:
  - speaker card contrast
  - one small คน support chip
  - one full introduction line under the cards
- Visual support choice: text-only

## Section 3 — Say where you are from with มาจาก
- Layout: image-anchored
- Learning focus: origin pattern plus country category
- Highlighted terms:
  - ประเทศ | bprà-thêet | country
  - มาจาก | maa-jàak | come from / be from
  - มาจากไทย | maa-jàak thai | be from Thailand
  - มาจากอังกฤษ | maa-jàak ang-grìt | be from England
  - มาจากอเมริกา | maa-jàak à-mee-rí-gaa | be from America
- On-screen plan:
  - ประเทศ category chip at the top
  - sentence frame card dominates the left panel
  - small flag or map cues under the country words
- Real-world image helpful: yes, but only lightly
- Search ideas:
  - simple Thailand England America flags flat lay
  - country flag icons Thailand England America

## Section 4 — Build a full two-line self-introduction
- Layout: dialogue-ladder
- Learning focus: stack greeting, name, country, and review cues
- Highlighted terms:
  - สวัสดีครับ/ค่ะ | sà-wàt-dii khráp/khâ | hello
  - ขอโทษ | khǎaw-thôot | excuse me / sorry
  - ฉันชื่อ... / ผมชื่อ... | chǎn/phǒm chûue... | my name is ...
  - ฉันมาจาก... / ผมมาจาก... | chǎn/phǒm maa-jàak... | I am from ...
  - ใช่ | châi | yes / that is right
  - ขอบคุณ | khàawp-khun | thank you
- On-screen plan:
  - four-step introduction ladder
  - small confirmation chip for ใช่
  - small closing thank-you chip
- Visual support choice: text-only

## Roleplay beat
- Show one turn at a time on the left side.
- Keep the question, country answer, and confirmation turns clearly separated.
- Let อังกฤษใช่ไหมคะ and ใช่ครับ appear as a tight confirmation pair.
- Do not let the country visual anchor expand into the right-third camera zone.
